stories.headline: 24 Fire near Fort Carson explodes to more than 1,000 acres, triggering mandatory evacuations near Penrose
stories.summary:
Fremont County officials ordered mandatory evacuations Friday afternoon for residents within two miles of Fremont County Road F45 and Colorado 115, including parts of Penrose, as the 24 Fire rapidly expanded to 1,067 acres on the southwest edge of Fort Carson. Residents, pets and livestock were directed to evacuate to Pathfinder Regional Park at 6639 Colorado 115, while those within three miles of the area were placed on pre-evacuation notice.

The fire started Wednesday on the east side of Highway 115 and was considered roughly 50 percent contained Thursday morning before strong winds caused it to jump containment lines and grow dramatically. As of Friday, the blaze had zero containment. Colorado 115 was closed in both directions between mile marker 18 near Penrose and mile marker 39 near Rock Creek Park.

Crews from El Paso County and several other agencies are assisting Fort Carson personnel. Army spokeswoman Dee McNutt said the fire is burning on military training land southeast of the main post and that no buildings are threatened. The cause of the fire has not been determined.

stories.headline: Denver expected to hit 90 degrees Saturday, shattering more all-time March records amid critical fire danger
stories.summary:
Denver is forecast to reach 90 degrees Saturday, which would break the daily record of 78 degrees and set a new all-time March high, surpassing the 85-degree mark recorded Thursday and Friday at Denver International Airport. A high of 90 would also mark the earliest 90-degree day ever recorded in Denver, well ahead of the average first 90-degree day on June 10 and the previous earliest occurrence on April 30, 1992.

A Fire Weather Warning is in effect for most of Colorado on Saturday, with winds forecast at 30 to 40 mph combining with extremely low humidity to create critical fire conditions across the Front Range, foothills and high mountain valleys. Meteorologists are urging residents to avoid any activity that could produce a spark.

A cold front is expected to arrive early Sunday, knocking temperatures back to the mid-60s, though no meaningful precipitation is forecast to accompany it. The reprieve will be short-lived, as high pressure is expected to rebuild next week with temperatures returning to the 80s and the potential for additional record highs Monday through Thursday.

stories.headline: Avalanche clinch playoff spot with 4-1 win at Chicago, first NHL team to secure postseason berth
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche became the first NHL team to clinch a playoff berth this season, securing the franchise's ninth consecutive postseason appearance with a 4-1 victory over the Chicago Blackhawks on Friday night. Goals from Martin Necas, Brock Nelson, Nazem Kadri and Valeri Nichushkin powered the win, which extended Colorado's Western Conference lead to four points over the Dallas Stars.

The clinching came after a rough stretch in which the Avalanche dropped four of five games, including a 7-2 home loss to Pittsburgh. Coach Jared Bednar said he was encouraged by how the team performed in a 2-1 shootout loss to Dallas earlier in the week, and credited the team's carry-over momentum into Chicago.

Colorado clinched in fewer than 70 games for the third time in franchise history during an 82-game season, previously accomplishing the feat in 1996-97 and 2000-01. The team also received good news on the injury front, with captain Gabriel Landeskog and forwards Artturi Lehkonen, Logan O'Connor and Ross Colton all traveling with the team and potentially returning as early as Sunday.

stories.headline: Jamal Murray scores 31, Nuggets hold off Raptors 121-115 to snap three-game losing streak
stories.summary:
Jamal Murray scored 31 points and Nikola Jokic hit a go-ahead jumper with 45 seconds remaining as the Denver Nuggets defeated the Toronto Raptors 121-115 Friday night at Ball Arena. Tim Hardaway Jr. added 23 points in the victory.

The win snapped a three-game losing streak for Denver, which entered the game at 42-28 under coach David Adelman. Jokic's clutch basket proved decisive as the Nuggets fended off a late Toronto push to secure the result.

The victory gives Denver needed momentum as the team pushes toward the postseason in what has been an adjustment period following roster and coaching changes. The Nuggets remain in playoff contention in the Western Conference standings.

stories.headline: Three men charged with second-degree murder in fatal beating outside southeast Denver nightclub
stories.summary:
Three men are facing second-degree murder charges after beating 47-year-old Neal Jones to death in the parking lot of the Ultra Lounge at 9755 E. Hampden Ave. in southeast Denver in the early morning hours of March 1. Denver police responded to a 911 assault call just after 2 a.m. and found Jones unresponsive; he was pronounced dead at HCA HealthONE Swedish hospital 30 minutes later.

According to an arrest affidavit, the confrontation began when a woman slapped another woman who was with Jones outside the club. Derek Wafford, 39; Demetrius Davis, 37; and Gregory Manard, 31, were with the woman who initiated the slap and began attacking Jones after he intervened. Surveillance video reviewed by police shows the three men hitting and kicking Jones while he was on the ground and placing him in a chokehold.

The medical examiner determined Jones died from a fractured skull and brain swelling caused by blunt force trauma. Davis and Manard are in custody on $100,000 bail, while Wafford was released on the same amount. Wafford also faces a misdemeanor assault charge; his next court appearance is Tuesday.

stories.headline: Longmont police officer fatally shoots woman who brandished firearm during mental health crisis response
stories.summary:
A Longmont police officer shot and killed a woman Thursday after she emerged from her garage brandishing a firearm following an hours-long standoff at a home in the 1500 block of East Third Avenue. Officers had originally responded at 4:15 p.m. to a report of a suicidal woman, and the situation escalated when she fired a weapon inside her garage as officers were preparing to leave.

Crisis negotiators, including officers trained in Crisis Intervention Team mental health response, spent an extended period attempting to persuade the woman to exit unarmed. The officer who fired was identified as a member of the department's SWAT team. The woman was transported to a local hospital, where she was pronounced dead.

The shooting prompted a shelter-in-place order for nearby residents that was lifted at 7:15 p.m. The officer involved has been placed on administrative leave per department policy, and the investigation will be conducted by the Boulder County District Attorney's Office Critical Incident Team. Neither the officer nor the woman has been publicly identified pending family notification.

stories.headline: Longmont teenager charged with first-degree murder in shooting death of stepbrother
stories.summary:
A teenager arrested Wednesday is facing a first-degree murder charge in the shooting death of his stepbrother at the Cloverbasin Village apartment complex in Longmont, according to the Boulder County District Attorney's Office. Police received a call at 3:53 p.m. from a boy who said he had shot and killed someone and that he was in possession of a Glock handgun, which he agreed to drop when officers arrived.

Officers found the victim in a bedroom with a gunshot wound and were unable to revive him; he was pronounced dead at the scene. Family members told police the two boys had a contentious relationship and had physically fought before. Multiple family members said they were unaware of any firearms in the home.

The ages of both boys were redacted in the arrest affidavit. A hearing will be held to determine whether the case will remain in juvenile court or whether the suspect will be tried as an adult. Boulder County District Attorney Michael Dougherty said prosecutors are working to determine how the teenager obtained the weapon and the appropriate path for prosecution.

stories.headline: Denver Book Society severs ties with co-owner Kwame Spearman following community backlash
stories.summary:
The Denver Book Society at 1700 N. Humboldt St. announced Friday that former Tattered Cover CEO Kwame Spearman has departed from its leadership following community backlash over his past actions. Building owner and founding partner Richard Garvin will now serve as the sole owner of the store, which had faced strain since its opening due to Spearman's involvement.

"Kwame is passionate about preserving independent bookstores and I'm appreciative of his early contributions," Garvin said in a news release announcing the change. The bookstore did not detail the specific nature of the community concerns that prompted the separation.

Spearman had previously served as CEO of Tattered Cover, Denver's prominent independent bookstore chain, before that role ended amid controversy. The Denver Book Society's future leadership structure and programming were not immediately detailed in Friday's announcement.

stories.headline: UCHealth and CU Anschutz invest in Google parent's AI health spinoff Verily
stories.summary:
UCHealth and the University of Colorado Anschutz Medical Campus have made an investment in Verily, the health technology spinoff of Google parent Alphabet, according to the Denver Business Journal. The investment follows a strategic collaboration the organizations announced in the fall of 2025.

The partnership signals a deepening commitment by two of Colorado's largest health care institutions to artificial intelligence-driven health tools. Verily focuses on applying data science and AI to disease management, research and health care delivery.

The financial terms of the investment were not disclosed. The move comes as health systems across the country accelerate AI adoption, and as Colorado's broader technology sector continues to attract significant health care innovation investment.

stories.headline: Aurora driver arrested after fleeing traffic stop and crashing into another vehicle
stories.summary:
An Aurora driver was arrested early Friday morning after allegedly fleeing a traffic stop and colliding with another vehicle, according to the Aurora Police Department. An officer attempted to stop the driver at Alameda Avenue and South Peoria Street at 5:06 a.m. for a traffic violation, but the driver continued westbound on Alameda before crashing at South Havana Street.

The driver attempted to flee a second time but was slowed by injuries sustained in the crash and was taken to a nearby hospital for evaluation. After being released from the hospital, the driver was booked into jail on charges of eluding and reckless driving. A passenger in the suspect's vehicle suffered minor injuries and was also treated at a hospital.

Aurora police did not release the identity of the suspect and did not report on the condition of anyone in the vehicle that was struck.

stories.headline: DCPA announces 2026-2027 Broadway season featuring 'Death Becomes Her,' 'The Great Gatsby,' and more
stories.summary:
The Denver Center for the Performing Arts has announced its 2026-2027 Broadway season, featuring a lineup that includes the stage adaptation of "Death Becomes Her" and "The Great Gatsby" musical among its headline productions. The announcement marks the DCPA's continued effort to bring major touring productions to the Denver metro area.

The season reflects the broader national trend of adapting popular film and literary properties for the stage, with both announced titles drawing from well-known source material. Additional productions included in the season were not detailed in available reporting.

Subscription and single-ticket information for the upcoming season was expected to be released by the DCPA. The organization is one of the largest nonprofit theater organizations in the United States and a major cultural anchor for the Denver metro area.

stories.headline: Denver's first licensed psilocybin microdosing cafe opens on South Broadway
stories.summary:
Denver's first licensed microdosing cafe has opened on South Broadway, offering psilocybin-assisted yoga and art classes in a regulated setting. The establishment represents a milestone in Denver's expanding legal psilocybin landscape following the city's 2019 decriminalization of the substance and subsequent state-level regulatory developments.

The cafe is designed around guided, experiential programming rather than traditional coffeehouse service, with offerings that pair low-dose psilocybin consumption with structured creative and wellness activities. The format reflects a growing national model for supervised therapeutic and recreational psychedelic use in licensed venues.

The opening comes as Colorado continues to build out its regulatory framework for psilocybin services, with Denver positioned as an early hub for legal access. Details on the business name, ownership and pricing structure were not immediately available.

weather.notable: About 33 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: My wife said I need to be more spontaneous. So I unplugged the WiFi without warning.

topStory.headline: Iran War Enters Fourth Week With Strikes on Nuclear Site, Gulf Energy Infrastructure and Diego Garcia Base
topStory.summary:
The conflict between Iran and the United States and Israel stretched into its fourth week with no signs of abating, as Iran's Natanz uranium enrichment complex was struck Friday and Iran launched ballistic missiles toward the U.S.-U.K. military base at Diego Garcia in the Indian Ocean. The International Atomic Energy Agency confirmed it had been informed of the Natanz attack and said no increase in off-site radiation levels had been detected, while IAEA Director General Rafael Grossi repeated a call for military restraint to prevent any risk of a nuclear accident. Iran's semiofficial Tasnim news agency reported no radioactive leak and said there was no danger to nearby residents, but condemned the strike as a violation of international nuclear safety and security regulations.

Iran continued to target Persian Gulf energy infrastructure in apparent retaliation for an Israeli strike on its South Pars gas field earlier in the week. Iranian drones and missiles struck Kuwait's Mina Al-Ahmadi refinery on both Thursday and Friday, sparking fires that forced officials to shut down portions of the facility. Iran also hit facilities in Qatar and Saudi Arabia, and QatarEnergy reported that its Ras Laffan Industrial City suffered extensive damage, with 17 percent of its liquefied natural gas capacity reduced — a loss the company estimated could cost $20 billion in revenue.

Israel said it was conducting a new wave of strikes targeting infrastructure in Tehran, having already hit more than 130 targets in the Iranian capital on Thursday. The Israel Defense Forces issued repeated warnings to the Israeli public of incoming missiles and said its defense systems were operational in response. The United States, meanwhile, is accelerating the deployment of additional Marines and sailors to the Middle East, according to two people familiar with the decision, as Secretary of State Marco Rubio held calls this week with foreign ministers from Japan, South Korea, the United Kingdom, France, Australia, the UAE, Kuwait, Qatar and Saudi Arabia, as well as the NATO Secretary General.

Russian President Vladimir Putin congratulated Iranian leaders on the Persian new year Nowruz and described Moscow as a loyal friend and reliable partner to Tehran, though some Iranian sources indicated they have received little concrete assistance from Russia during the crisis. Putin also condemned the killing of Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei as a cynical murder and said U.S. and Israeli strikes had thrust the Middle East into an abyss and triggered a major global energy crisis. The extent of actual Russian support for Iran remains disputed.

The back-and-forth strikes on oil and nuclear infrastructure have raised alarms about the potential for full regional war and global economic disruption. Oil prices have surged in response to damage at major Gulf facilities, and President Donald Trump stated that Israel would not again attack the South Pars gas field unless Iran struck Qatar again. With no diplomatic off-ramp publicly visible and military operations intensifying on multiple fronts, analysts and world leaders have warned that the risk of further escalation remains high.
